coredata.dc:title: Unilateral RF sensors based on parallel-plate architecture for improved surface-scan MRI analysis of commercial pouch cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Romanenko, Konstantin
Avdievich, Nikolai
coredata.dc:description: Rapid expansion of the Li-ion pouch cell market is driven by the looming problem of permanent depletion of natural gas reservoirs and by the growing demand for high-performance portable devices and electric vehicles. Safety and performance of Li-ion cells have been two main focal points of the extensive battery research. Surface-scan Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an operando method designed for the accurate detection of substandard battery cells and for monitoring electrochemical processes with high spatial and temporal resolutions. Intercalation-dependent magnetism and charge transfer processes in the cell's electrodes give rise to characteristic magnetic field patterns outside the cell. For accurate mapping of such patterns, we proposed the concept of a unilateral radio-frequency (RF) sensor, a flat thin resonator encapsulating a proton-rich solid-state detection medium. When the pouch cell is placed in direct contact with the sensor, the magnetic field patterns propagate inside the detection medium, and the corresponding spatial distribution of Larmor precession frequencies can be detected with MRI. In this work, we developed and evaluated a series of RF sensor configurations based on parallel-plate architecture enhanced by arrays of distributed capacitors. The parallel-plate approach does not suffer from RF interference with pouch cells and provides excellent sensitivity and B 1-field homogeneity. The optimal configuration of the parallel-plate sensor depends on the dimensions of the pouch cell and the distribution of parallel capacitors. This article includes the results of experimental tests, RF-field simulations, and strategies to further improve the surface-scan MRI method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Unilateral RF sensor
Magnetic field mapping
Inside-out MRI
SPRITE
Battery diagnostics
State-of-charge
State-of-health

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Resonance properties and dimensions of the parallel-plate sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Graduate School of Chemistry of the Université Paris-Saclay - ‘ France 2030’ ANR-11-IDEX-0003 , and by the ANR PIA fund : ANR-20-IDEES-0002 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A depiction of the Surface-scan MRI sensor (front and back side, X-Z view) based on the parallel-plate architecture. The front plate is the working surface of the sensor placed in contact with the pouch cell. Two arrays of capacitors (top and bottom, N C = 10) connect two thin (ca. 60 μm) copper sheets separated by a thin (ca. 1 mm) layer of proton-rich polymer. Variable capacitors for tuning and matching are incorporated on the back side of the sensor. A circuit diagram of the sensor is shown on the right. (b) Local representation of magnetic field perturbations propagating into the detection medium. (c) Surface-scan MRI image (left) of an 'iPhone-5S' pouch cell (right) registered in a 3T field. The image corresponds to an area (ca. 34 × 65 mm2) indicated with a blue rectangle. Strong dipolar patterns form at the tip of the extended Ni-plated tab and at the terminals (indicated with white rectangles).

(a, b) Simulated maps of B 1 field (X-Z view, averaged along the Y axis) for 70 × 70 × 1 and 40 × 70 × 1 mm3 sensor configurations, respectively. N C values are indicated near each image. (c) B 1 distribution parameters depending on N C. Red traces – configuration 70 × 70 × 1 mm3; Blue traces – configuration 40 × 70 × 1 mm3. Top: mean (○) and local (×) B 1 values at the geometric centers of the models; Bottom: (□) root-mean-square deviations (σ).

(a) Photographs of a solenoidal sensor prototype (top) and the 'iPhone-6' cell placed in contact with the sensor (bottom). (b) T 2*-weighted B 1 - modulated density image of the silicone detection medium. (c,d) Maps of B 1 distribution inside the solenoidal sensor without and with the pouch cell attached, respectively. (e) Magnetic field perturbations at the surface of the 'iPhone-6' pouch cell in the 3T field. ROIs shown in (b-e) correspond to an area ca. 71 × 71 mm2.

Photographs of the 'W' N C = 18 (a) and 'N' N C = 18 (b) sensor configurations. Left: working surface; Middle: backside containing tuning and matching capacitors; Right: 'One Plus 7 Pro' (a) and 'iPhone-6' (b) battery cells placed in contact with the working surfaces of the sensors.

(a-d) Maps of B 1 distribution (Eq. (5)) inside 'W' (N C = 6, 18, and 34), and 'N' (N C = 18) sensor configurations, respectively. (e-h) Magnetic field perturbations (Eq. (7)) produced by 'One Plus 7 Pro' and 'iPhone-6' pouch cells (as indicated above each map) measured with 'W' (N C = 6, 18, and 34), and 'N' (N C = 18) sensors, respectively. ROIs correspond to an area of 71 × 71 mm2.
(a) B 1 map of the 'W' (N C = 34) sensor in contact with the 'One Plus 7 Pro' cell (b). B 1 data are corrupted near the tips of the extended tab and terminals indicated with red rectangles in (b).

B DC maps of a fully charged 'iPhone-5S' cell operating at a current magnitude of 2 A (left - charging; right - discharging). Measurements were performed using the 'N' (N C = 18) sensor. The position of ROI (34 × 65 mm2) with respect to the cell is indicated with a blue rectangle on the right. Positions of the terminals and the extended tab are highlighted with white rectangles.
Simulation parameters of RF sensors based on parallel-plate architecture.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: B 1° – magnitude of the RF field at the center of the sensor (per 1 W of stimulated power); B 1 a – average B 1 magnitude; σ – root-mean-square deviation.
B 1° – magnitude of the RF field at the center of the sensor (per 1 W of transmitter power at the resonator input); B 1 a – average B 1 magnitude; σ – root-mean-square deviation.

head.ce:title: Unilateral RF sensors based on parallel-plate architecture for improved surface-scan MRI analysis of commercial pouch cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CEA - NIMBE, CNRS, Université Paris-Saclay, Gif-sur-Yvette 91191, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCEA, CNRS, NIMBE, Université Paris-Saclay, Gif-sur-Yvette 91191, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Max-Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics, Tübingen 72076, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMax-Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics, Tübingen 72076, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid expansion of the Li-ion pouch cell market is driven by the looming problem of permanent depletion of natural gas reservoirs and by the growing demand for high-performance portable devices and electric vehicles. Safety and performance of Li-ion cells have been two main focal points of the extensive battery research. Surface-scan Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an operando method designed for the accurate detection of substandard battery cells and for monitoring electrochemical processes with high spatial and temporal resolutions. Intercalation-dependent magnetism and charge transfer processes in the cell's electrodes give rise to characteristic magnetic field patterns outside the cell. For accurate mapping of such patterns, we proposed the concept of a unilateral radio-frequency (RF) sensor, a flat thin resonator encapsulating a proton-rich solid-state detection medium. When the pouch cell is placed in direct contact with the sensor, the magnetic field patterns propagate inside the detection medium, and the corresponding spatial distribution of Larmor precession frequencies can be detected with MRI. In this work, we developed and evaluated a series of RF sensor configurations based on parallel-plate architecture enhanced by arrays of distributed capacitors. The parallel-plate approach does not suffer from RF interference with pouch cells and provides excellent sensitivity and B 1-field homogeneity. The optimal configuration of the parallel-plate sensor depends on the dimensions of the pouch cell and the distribution of parallel capacitors. This article includes the results of experimental tests, RF-field simulations, and strategies to further improve the surface-scan MRI method.

ce:keyword.ce:text: single point ramped imaging with T 1 enhancement

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The relevance of portable energy storage technologies is underpinned by disputes on global warming and the rapidly growing electric vehicle market. The energy density of Li-ion cells is pushed to the limits which can result in unsafe or unpredictable behavior of battery packs. Despite the significant progress made thus far, our understanding of chemical phenomena taking place in Li-ion and other promising types of cells remains incomplete. Li-ion battery research entails an extensive array of characterization techniques, ranging from sophisticated electrochemical analyses to advanced imaging and spectroscopic methods, all aimed at comprehensively investigating the intricate properties and behaviors of these energy storage systems. Methods based on nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR and MRI) have proven to be exceptionally informative, providing unparalleled fundamental insights into the materials chemistry and electrochemical phenomena [1,2]. These studies however are limited to custom-built cell prototypes designed for improved compatibility with magnetic resonance methods. Operando magnetic resonance studies of commercial pouch cells have been virtually impossible due to negligible radio frequency (RF) field penetration, ultimate line-broadening, and strong magnetism. Surface-scan MRI is a method [3–6] developed with a specific focus on commercial pouch cells, offering great potential for advancing their characterization and analysis. This highly promising approach is based on 2D Centric-Scan Single Point Ramped Imaging with T 1 Enhancement (SPRITE) [7]. Surface-scan MRI leverages magnetic field mapping in the detection medium layer placed near the cell's surface. The magnetic field map can show contributions originating from state-of-charge (SoC) dependent magnetic susceptibility (χ) variations (B SoC) and the coherent motion of charged particles in the cell (B DC). A conceptual breakthrough in sensitivity enhancement has recently been made through the introduction of the unilateral RF sensor, a flat solenoid that encloses the polymer detection medium [6]. Due to an optimized filling factor (∼1), such a design offered a factor of ∼4 improvement in signal-to-noise (SNR) ratio as compared to conventional birdcage coils.

The solenoidal sensor has two main drawbacks: inhomogeneous B 1, which is evident in a periodically modulated 1H signal magnitude [6], and RF interference with the pouch cell. The latter is due to the reflection of the RF field from the conductive casing of the cell. This RF shield effect depends on the solenoid configuration and can result in a reduced B 1 magnitude and poor SNR in regions of interest. The presence of such flaws in the sensor's performance necessitates the consideration of the non-uniform SNR distribution during surface-scan MRI data analysis.

To address this challenge, we propose RF sensor designs based on a parallel-plate transmission line architecture, Fig. 1 a. Such a configuration inherently shields the volume occupied by the detection medium from interference with external conductive objects. Note, that parallel plates of the resonator do not prevent the constant magnetic field perturbations from extending into the polymer detection medium. Several noteworthy studies demonstrated the use of parallel-plate NMR resonators for probing chemical processes in Nafion membranes of operating fuel cells [8,9] and in model Li-ion cells adapted for sufficient RF-field penetration [10–12]. Surface-scan MRI experiments equipped with improved RF sensors would enable reliable quality control applications and new insights into fundamental processes in commercial pouch cells and model electrochemical systems. In this work, we introduce a strategy for the optimization of sensitivity and B 1 homogeneity in surface-scan MRI sensors based on parallel-plate architecture.

To effectively adapt the concept of the parallel-plate resonator to surface-scan MRI applications, it is crucial to consider factors defining the sensitivity of the RF probe: (a) Resonance properties and dimensions that determine SNR and transmit performance; (b) Dimensions of the pouch cell; (c) Properties of the detection medium; (d) Magnitude of the polarizing magnetic field B 0. In this study, we focus on factors (a) and (b), and examine factors (c) and (d) to some extent. Fig. 1a presents an illustrative overview of the surface-scan MRI sensor based on the parallel-plate architecture. A thin layer of proton-rich polymer separates two copper sheets. Arrays of parallel capacitors connect the copper sheets at the top and bottom. Each side of the sensor contains N C/2 symmetrically placed capacitors. The flat front side (the working surface) allows tight contact with the surface of the pouch cell. The backside contains tuning and matching circuits and incorporates a connection to the 50 Ω coaxial cable. An RF circuit diagram is shown on the right. The sensor is stabilized with a rigid fiberglass PCB support mountable on a homemade probe body.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resonance properties and dimensions of the parallel-plate sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the principle of reciprocity [13,14], the sensitivity of an RF probe scales with the B 1 magnitude per unit current. Therefore, both the transmit efficiency (B 1 P−
1/2, where P is the RF power at the resonator input) and local SNR are proportional ∼ (ηQV S −1)1/2 [15], where η is the filling factor, Q is the quality factor, and V S is the sample volume. Therefore, in general, to improve the sensor performance, one must increase the Q-factor and minimize the resonator (sample) volume. To maximize the transmit efficiency of the RF sensor, the entire volume of the resonator should be occupied by the detection medium (η = 1). The Q-factor (Eq. (1)) is a function of the resonance frequency (ω), resistance (R) defined by combined resonator and sample losses, and the effective inductance (L) of the sensor largely determined by its dimensions:
(1)
Q
=
ω L
R
− 1
.

The distribution of capacitors (Fig. 1a) is another important optimization parameter. The quality factor of an RF capacitor is known to decrease with the capacitance value [16]. Thus, we expect to increase the Q-factor of the RF sensor while improving the B 1 field homogeneity by increasing the number of capacitors (N C).

Li-ion pouch cells are available in a diverse range of sizes and form factors. In terms of sensitivity, spatial resolution, cost-effectiveness, and simplicity of the tuning-matching system, it is conceivable that the optimal dimensions of the sensor (D X and D Z) should be cell-specific. It will be demonstrated below that matching the D X and D Z dimensions of the sensor to the corresponding dimensions of specific pouch cell models leads to an optimal B 1 magnitude across the entire face of the cell.

The proton density and the relaxation time constants (T 2 and T 1) are the key characteristics of the detection medium that significantly influence the sensitivity of surface-scan MRI. Silicone is an excellent choice of material for sensor prototyping due to its exceptional mechanical characteristics, heat resistance, and availability. However, silicone rubbers typically have T 1’s ∼ 1 s which is not the optimal choice in terms of temporal resolution. In the previous work, we discovered that a PVC-based material (Plastisol, 'GoldenGrub') [4–6], exhibits a much shorter T 1 (∼70 ms). In this study, we examine and compare resonance characteristics of RF sensor prototypes based on silicone and Plastisol.

The optimal polymer thickness is determined by the intricate interplay among B 1 magnitude, SNR, desirable spatial resolution, and manufacturability factors. An increase in the layer thickness favorably affects the signal-to-noise ratio but leads to blurring of the magnetic field patterns due to data averaging in the direction perpendicular to the polymer layer [17]. In the previous experiments with an isotropic spatial resolution of ∼ 1.2 mm, we established that a 1 mm thick silicone layer provides acceptable image quality without the need for lengthy signal averaging [4–6]. Also, a standard thickness of 1 mm is well-suited for the manufacturability aspect of the surface-scan MRI technology, particularly, in terms of mechanical properties.

Perturbations of the polarizing field B 0 induced within a pouch cell propagate into the polymer detection medium, Fig. 1b. The sensitivity of surface-scan MRI to magnetic field contrast originating from susceptibility variations ∆χ inside the cell scales with ∆χ B 0. Therefore, the use of high magnetic fields can be beneficial for the overall sensitivity of the method. However, battery components such as extended tabs and terminals contain bulk metals (Ni and Fe). Strong dipolar patterns form at the tips of extended Ni-plated tabs and near terminals [3]. As a result, the inhomogeneity of the magnetic field can approach several hundred parts per million (ppm) giving rise to local T 2* values (∼ ∆χ −1 B 0 −1) on the order of tens of microseconds. An MRI technique suitable for accurate mapping of strongly inhomogeneous magnetic fields is based on purely phase-encoded k-space sampling (Centric-Scan SPRITE) as described in Refs. [3–6]. Unlike the frequency-encoded and slice-selective MRI protocols [18,19], SPRITE images are completely free of misregistration ('susceptibility' and 'chemical shift') artifacts. Fig. 1c shows a pattern of magnetic field perturbation produced by a fully charged commercial pouch cell (iPhone-5S) placed in a 7T field. One of the main advantages of SPRITE imaging is the ultra-short phase encoding time capability (T P ∼ 10 - 100 μs) that prevents significant signal attenuation and justifies the use of high magnetic fields (≥ 7T) in surface-scan MRI applications.

Fig. 2 shows B 1 field simulations for 'Wide' 70(X) × 70(Z) × 1(Y) mm3 (a) and 'Narrow'40(X) × 70(Z) × 1(Y) mm3 (b) sensor configurations with N C = 6, 10, 14, 18, and 34, and the stimulated input power of 1 W. For the 'Wide' configuration (Fig. 2a), Q-factor, B 1 magnitude, and homogeneity improve by nearly a factor of 2 in the examined N C range. Total RF losses in a resonator are a combination of losses in the polymer and in individual capacitors. Thus, a set of smaller-value capacitors (connected in parallel) can provide improved resonance characteristics as compared to a smaller set of larger-value capacitors. Note that the total capacitance values in our models correspond to the same resonance frequency (300.4 MHz).

The 'Narrow' configuration (Fig. 2b) generates approximately 60 % stronger B 1 field than the 'Wide' one. Reducing the width of the sensor improves its Q factor due to the effect of increased inductance (Eq. (1)).

The statistical analysis of B 1 distributions is summarized in Fig. 2c. The plots show average, B1
a, and local (at the centers of sensors), B 1°, magnitudes of RF field (per 1 W of stimulated power), Fig. 2c (top). Root-mean-square deviations (σ) of B 1 distributions describe the B 1 homogeneity, Fig. 2c (bottom).

Note that the maximum numbers of capacitors (N C) achievable for 'Narrow' and 'Wide' configurations (based on a 3 mm wide 500-V capacitor) are close to 26 and 46, respectively. In this study, we limited our simulations to practically achievable configurations with N C's of 18 and 34 for 'Narrow' and 'Wide' configurations, respectively. Parameters of the CST models are provided in Table 1 (dimensions, N C, capacitors' values (C), Q-factor, and B 1 field distribution metrics).

The thickness of the resonator (D Y) influences both the sample volume (V S) and the Q-factor. B 1 simulations performed for 'Wide' sensor models with 0.5, 1, and 2 mm plate separations and 18 capacitors show that the distance between the plates has a negligible (less than ∼2 %) effect on the B 1 magnitude distribution (Table 1). This result is consistent with the fact that an increase in the sample volume (V S) matches an increase in the Q-factor rendering the corresponding B 1 unchanged [15].

The RF field simulations (Fig. 2) reveal that the distribution of capacitive elements can significantly increase the performance of parallel plate resonators in terms of B 1 magnitude and homogeneity. Importantly, the simulations suggest that for optimal performance, sensors should be custom-tailored to align with the dimensions of specific battery cell models. Indeed, as shown above, reducing the horizontal size of the sensor (D X) leads to a substantial increase in sensitivity. At the same time, the ability to scan the entire surface of the pouch cell within one measurement is beneficial for high temporal resolution of the method. Thus, for the optimal performance (expressed as the product of sensor sensitivity and temporal resolution) the sensor dimensions (D X, D Z) should match the corresponding dimensions of the cell.

A quantitative B 1 map of an RF resonator can be obtained using a SPRITE-based approach similar to one described by Vashaee et al. [20]. In an approximation involving a low flip angle excitation, we can express the SPRITE image intensity (I) as:
(2)
I = A
B 1
2
P ,
where P is the duration of the RF excitation pulse, and A is a constant proportional to the product of the local nuclear magnetization (M 0) and an attenuation factor (F) arising from the transverse spin relaxation during the phase encoding period. This approach relies on the assumption that the detection medium has uniform spatial distributions of proton density, T 2 and T 2*. The B 1 map can be reconstructed as follows:
(3)
B 1
=
A
− 1 / 2
(
d
I /
d
P )
1 / 2
.

The matrix dI / dP is determined experimentally. Summation of Eq. (3) over the image space gives:
(4)
B 1
a
=
N
− 1
A
− 1 / 2
Σ
(
d
I /
d
P )
1 / 2
.
Here, B 1 a is an average B 1 field calculated over N image pixels. B 1 a can be measured independently in a conventional nutation NMR experiment. Thus, a combination of Eqs. (3) and (4) leads to an expression for B 1:
(5)
B 1
=
B 1
a
N
(
d
I /
d
P )
1 / 2
(
Σ
(
d
I /
d
P )
1 / 2
)
− 1
.

Flat solenoidal RF sensors have been developed previously to improve the sensitivity of surface-scan MRI battery diagnostics, Fig. 3 a. Despite the demonstrated high performance [6], two major drawbacks of the solenoidal architecture have been identified: 1. Inhomogeneous B 1 distribution resulting in a periodic intensity pattern (Fig. 3b,c). 2. RF interference with a pouch cell (Fig. 3d) degrading the B 1 homogeneity. This effect depends on specific parameters of the solenoid (e.g., the gap between turns and width of the conductive tape) and the cell. Disadvantageous effects of the RF interference are evident from the comparison of B 1 maps measured for the sensor without and with the attached cell, Fig. 3c and d, respectively. Dipolar patterns of magnetic field perturbation (ca. ±150 ppm) produced by Ni and Fe-containing components of the pouch cell are shown in Fig. 3e. Note that the inhomogeneous B 1 field modulates SNR across the surface-scan MRI image but it does not affect the true values of measurable perturbations of the polarizing field B 0 (e.g., metrics B SoC and B DC [5,6]). A low-quality region of the B 1 map (Fig. 3d) near the center of the image arises at the tip of the extended Ni-plated tab. It is likely that in addition to the low SNR at this location, the RF excitation can be selective due to the presence of a strong magnetic field gradient.

Strong homogeneous B 1 is the key to accurate measurements of B SoC and B DC components. Subtle variations in these metrics can indicate the presence of various defects in cells such as mechanical damage or electrochemical degradation associated with violation of standard operational protocols [3–6,17]. Increased accuracy of surface-scan MRI data in the region close to the cell's terminals is of particular importance. In this region, the B SoC and B DC metrics significantly deviate from respective average values, thus indicating the non-uniform distribution of cathode lithiation and enhanced degradation of the active cathode material.

As demonstrated by numerical simulations, the parallel-plate RF architecture can provide a dramatically improved B 1 homogeneity. To validate these results, we constructed two series of parallel-plate sensor prototypes with 1 mm thick silicone and Plastisol sheets. The silicone-based series consists of the 'W' configuration 70 × 75 × 1 mm3 with N C = 6, 18 and 34, and the 'N' configuration 44 × 75 × 1 mm3 with N C = 18. The Plastisol-based series consists of the 'W' configuration 70 × 75 × 1 mm3 with N C = 18 and 34, and the 'N' configuration 44 × 75 × 1 mm3 with N C = 18. Electronic components and resonance characteristics of sensor prototypes are provided in Table 2 . The sensors were tested via RF return loss (S11) analysis, B 1 field mapping, as described in Section 3.2., and surface-scan MRI visualization of commercial pouch cells.

As discussed above, cell-specific dimensions of the unilateral RF sensor can ensure the optimal sensitivity of surface-scan MRI measurements. Following this principle, the engineered sensors are apt for analyzing pouch cells with X-Z dimensions similar to those of the 'One Plus 7 Pro' (60 × 75 mm2) and 'iPhone-6' (40 × 95 mm2). Fig. 4 shows the photographs of 'W' and 'N' prototypes with N C = 18.

In general, the improvement of B 1 distribution with N C and D X −1 (Fig. 5 a-d, Table 2) is in line with the simulations. From the experimental tests, it is evident that the performance of the 'W' configuration improves markedly both in terms of the B 1 magnitude and homogeneity (Table 2).

The 'N' prototype (N C = 18) is intended for analysis of cells with low D X: DZ aspect ratio (e.g., 'iPhone-6' model). The narrow configuration performs better than any of the 'W' prototypes (Table 2). This underscores the idea of the custom approach to sensor design based on specific pouch cell dimensions.

Note that the simulations were intended to demonstrate general trends (B 1 vs N C and D X −1) rather than the accurate reproduction of the experimental data. The simulated and experimental B 1 values deviate by up to ∼30 % due to several non-matching parameters of model and prototype resonators, i.e., sensor dimensions (D Z, plate thickness), dimensions and Q-factors of capacitors, and dielectric characteristics of polymers. In simulations, the EM properties of the medium between the parallel plates (ε = 3, tanδ = 0.005) were adapted from the literature [21] and may not match these of the actual detection media.

In terms of Q-factors, the sensors constructed with Plastisol show inferior performance to silicone-based sensors by 4 - 15 % (Table 2). However, the short-T 1 Plastisol is beneficial for high throughput screening applications as it enables an order of magnitude faster signal averaging (i.e., improved SNR).

A surface-scan MRI image is a map of local magnetic field offsets (∆B) calculated from the temporal evolution of the MRI signal phase φ(T P
k ):
(6)
Δ
B =
Δ
φ
( r ,
T
P
)
Δ
T
P
− 1
γ
− 1
,
where T P
k = T P 0 + (k-1) SW −1 is the phase encoding period corresponding to a k th data point, T P 0 is the minimum phase encoding delay (a period preceding the signal acquisition), γ is the gyromagnetic ratio of the proton, and SW is a sampling bandwidth.

The measurement of the net magnetic field perturbation produced by the cell involves two independent MRI experiments (with and without the cell placed in contact with the sensor, ∆B cell and ∆B b, respectively):
(7)
Δ
B
cell
−
Δ
B
b
.
The latter term is the 'reference' map representing the background magnetic field not associated with the cell.

Surface-scan MRI images of 'One Plus 7 Pro' and 'iPhone-6' pouch cells (rest state, SoC = 100 %) acquired with 'W' (N C = 6, 18, and 34) and 'N' (N C = 18) sensors are shown in Fig. 5e-g and h, respectively. In these images, the accuracy is modulated by the B 1 distributions, Fig. 5a-d, respectively. Note that the ∆B maps measured with the 'W' sensors (Fig. 5e-g) appear nearly identical. However, the corresponding B 1 distributions (Fig. 5a-c) indicate that the most accurate ∆B data in that series are delivered by the 'W' (N C = 34) sensor (Fig. 5g). The improved B 1 homogeneity is beneficial for the analysis of surface-scan MRI metrics near the tabs of pouch cells where electrochemical processes are more dynamic [4–6].

Effects of RF interference with pouch cells are negligible as demonstrated for the 'W' (N C = 34) sensor in contact with the 'One Plus 7 Pro' cell, Fig. 6
. It is worth noting, however, that near the tips of the highly magnetic metal components, B 1 measurements are affected by strong T 2* - weighting (low SNR in magnitude images). In addition, although the RF excitation pulses are supposed to be non-selective (hard) throughout the detection medium, the strong magnetic field gradients at these specific locations can require shorter pulses.

Operando surface-scan MRI experiments provide a novel imaging contrast suitable for battery health assessment (defect detection) in commercial pouch cells. In the previous work, we introduced a B DC metric associated with the motion of charges (electrons and ions) through the electrodes of the cell [5,6]. In this case, the ∆B measurement is referenced with respect to the magnetic field of the cell in the rest state at the same state of charge:
(8)
B DC
=
Δ
B
DC
−
Δ
B
rest
.

B DC maps of an iPhone-5S cell operating in charge and discharge modes are shown in Fig. 7 . These experiments were performed with the 'N' (N C = 18) sensor. Note, that the current density varies over the jelly-roll electrodes leading to a non-uniform B DC distribution.

Improving the sensitivity as well as spatial and temporal resolutions of surface-scan MRI method can enable advanced high throughput battery quality control and provide new insights into electrochemical energy storage phenomena [22]. An optimal design of the unilateral RF sensor is a pivotal element enabling sensitive surface-scan MRI measurements. The parallel-plate RF architecture is advantageous for surface-scan MRI applications due to minimized effects of RF interference with pouch cells. In this work, we conducted systematic numerical simulations and MRI measurements of B 1 field distributions in parallel-plate RF sensor prototypes. Our systematic study shows that the sensor's Q-factor, and B 1 magnitude and homogeneity scale with the number of distributed capacitors. For optimal performance, sensors should be custom-tailored to align with the dimensions of specific pouch cell models. To improve the sensor performance further, one should seek low-loss, high-proton density, short-T 1 detection media, and high-Q capacitors. While the even distribution of capacitance leads to an excellent B 1 homogeneity and magnitude, a desirable B 1 pattern can be designed through fine adjustment of values and positions of individual capacitors. Thus, it is possible to focus B 1 near the areas of the cell's surface that represent a particular interest. Such selective enhancement in sensitivity can be achieved through the implementation of arrays of variable capacitors.

MRI experiments were performed using a Bruker Avance NEO system equipped with a 7 T vertical Super-Wide-Bore magnet (I.D. 154 mm) and a 3D set of water-cooled gradient coils (79 G∙cm−1 at 200 A). Custom RF sensors were manufactured with either silicone or Plastisol (Golden Grub- Hard Formula, Hazardiscs) sheets 1 mm thick, and 60 μm thick copper tapes. Tuning and matching of sensors to 50 Ω at the 1H resonance frequency of 300.4 MHz were carried out with a combination of non-magnetic fixed (Exxelia, Paris, France; Voltage rating, 500 V) and variable capacitors (model, 52H02, Johanson Corp., Boonton, NJ, USA; Capacity range, 1.5 − 19 pF; Voltage rating, 1.25 kV).

A custom 2D Centric-Scan SPRITE protocol [3,6] was designed in the ParaVision 360 environment. The RF power was set to 10 W in all experiments, and RF pulse durations varied depending on the sensor configuration and the type of experiment. The magnetization flip angles did not exceed π/36. The sequence consisted of hard RF pulses (flip angle ca. 5°) separated by repetition intervals T R ca. 1.1 ms. T R included a minimum phase encoding delay (T P 0, 58 µs), data sampling at a rate of 250 kHz for 32 µs, and gradient switching and stabilization intervals each 0.5 ms long. A k-space matrix (64 × 64) was assembled from eight sectoral trajectories constructed on the Cartesian grid as demonstrated in Refs. [3,23]. The interleaved acquisition employed magnetization recovery delays of 5 or 0.5 s depending on the material of the detection medium (silicone or Plastisol, respectively). Eight complex data points sampled per gradient step gave rise to a set of eight purely phase-encoded images with T P's in the range from 59 to 90 µs. The imaging plane is aligned with the polymer detection medium layer of the unilateral RF sensor. A spatial resolution of 1.2 mm was achieved with a gradient magnitude of 0.139 T/m. The k-space data were chirp Z-transformed to the spatial domain using an algorithm [7] implemented in MATLAB 7.0 (The MathWorks, Inc.).

Surface-scan MRI maps were calculated from the temporal evolution of phase images as described in Refs. [3,6]. Accumulation of a single data set 8 × 64 × 64 takes 44 and 7 s with silicone- and Plastisol-based sensors, respectively. Experiments described by Eqs. (5), (7) and (8) were performed with 8, 4, and 2 signal averages, respectively.

B 1 maps were reconstructed from a series of Centric-Scan SPRITE images (magnitude mode) as described by Eqs. (2)–(5). RF pulse durations were adjusted so that the magnetization flip angles were in the range from 1⁰ to 5⁰ at an amplifier power of 10 W. A pixel-by-pixel linear regression algorithm was implemented with a Matlab function 'polyfit'.
EM simulations were performed using the time-domain solver of CST Studio Suite 2021 (Dassault Systèmes, Vélizy-Villacoublay, France), which is based on the finite-integration technique [24].

Simulations were performed for two resonator configurations with plate dimensions 70 × 70 mm2 ('Wide') and 40 × 70 mm2 ('Narrow'). The distance between the plates was set to 1 mm. Due to the skin effect (skin depth ∼ 4 μm at 300 MHz), the thickness of the plates could be set to a value (0.3 mm) larger than the experimental one (60 μm). This enabled a simplified model meshing (0.1 mm isotropic mesh) without any effects on the results of simulations. The ports were evenly distributed on two sides of the resonator as shown in Fig. 1a.

The 'Wide' model was built with 6, 10, 14, 18, and 34 ports, while the 'Narrow' model with 6, 10, 14, and 18 ports. The 'Wide' model with 18 ports was also simulated with plate separations of 0.5 mm and 2 mm. Capacitors were connected to each port in the postprocessing step. Losses in the capacitors were taken into account via added series resistors R c (0.055 Ohm) and series inductors L c (0.56 nH) known from the product specifications.

The model resonators were tuned and matched in the postprocessing step using the capacitive network shown in Fig. 1a. EM properties of silicone rubber (ε = 3, tanδ = 0.005) were adapted from Howard [21]. These values, however, may not exactly match the properties of the polymer used in our experimental sensor prototypes.

RF sensor performance was evaluated based on the simulations of a circularly polarized field at 1 W of stimulated RF power at the resonator input. Q-factors of the resonators were measured from the S 11 frequency dependence as twice the ratio of the resonance frequency over the 3-dB bandwidth [25].

ce:list-item.ce:para: 'iPhone-5S': Model 1ICP6/34/91; Capacity 1560 mAh; Charge voltage 4.3 V; Nominal voltage 3.8 V; Standard GB31241–2014; Cell dimensions (mm): 90 × 32 × 3.7 (±0.1).
'iPhone-6': Model 1ICP4/39/96; Capacity 1810 mAh; Charge voltage 4.35 V; Nominal voltage 3.82 V; Standard GB31241–2014; Cell dimensions (mm): 97.6 × 38.6 × 3.1 (±0.1).

'One Plus 7 Pro': Model BLP685 1ICP5/62/74; Rated capacity 3610 mAh (13.89 Wh); Typical capacity 3700 mAh (14.24 Wh); Charge voltage 4.4 V; Nominal voltage 3.85 V; Cell dimensions (mm): 75 × 60 × 4.6 (±0.1).

ce:section.ce:para: Cell operation modes were controlled with a BTS-4008–5V6A-S1 system (NEWARE, Hong Kong, China).

Konstantin Romanenko: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nikolai Avdievich: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Graduate School of Chemistry of the Université Paris-Saclay - 'France 2030' ANR-11-IDEX-0003, and by the ANR PIA fund: ANR-20-IDEES-0002.
